title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - All Critical Infrastructure
description: Curated OT/ICS cybersecurity advisories, incidents and threat intelligence from primary sources. Editorial summaries by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

_otthreatfeed.usage: Free to reuse with attribution. Credit 'Mike Holcomb & UtilSec' and link back to otthreatfeed.com and to each item's linked primary source. Summaries are editorial paraphrase - verify against the primary source before acting.

items.title: “FortiBleed” credential-theft campaign hits tens of thousands of FortiGate devices — the edge firewalls and VPNs guarding OT networks
items.content_text: CISA is warning that a campaign dubbed FortiBleed harvested working administrator and VPN credentials from internet-facing FortiGate firewalls and SSL-VPN gateways at scale — leaked credentials tie to roughly 74,000 exposed Fortinet devices, part of a far larger operation that collected over 110 million credentials, with active exploitation confirmed and IoCs shared via DHS. The escalation this cycle: researchers (SOCRadar) link the stolen credentials directly to the INC Ransom and Lynx ransomware operations — a single operator seen logged into both groups' negotiation panels, overlapping victims, and at least 12 ransomware deployments encrypting hundreds of endpoints. Why OT defenders should care: these appliances are the front door to industrial networks, terminating the remote access and IT/OT segmentation plants depend on, so stolen admin credentials hand attackers — now including ransomware crews — a straight path toward control systems without touching a single PLC.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: CISA alert 18 Jun 2026 — DHS IoCs 2 Jul; ransomware linkage confirmed; ongoing

items.title: “CitrixBleed” returns: NetScaler flaw (CVE-2026-8451) exploited within 24 hours, leaking secrets from the gateways that front OT remote access
items.content_text: Citrix patched CVE-2026-8451 on 30 June — an unauthenticated memory-overread in NetScaler ADC and Gateway appliances configured as a SAML identity provider — and researchers observed in-the-wild exploitation within 24 hours, aided by same-day public proof-of-concept from watchTowr. Echoing the original CitrixBleed, the flaw lets a remote attacker read appliance memory and harvest live session tokens, cleartext credentials and TLS private keys straight from an HTTP response. Like FortiGate (see top story), NetScaler gateways commonly terminate the remote access and IT/OT segmentation that plants depend on, so leaked secrets can hand an attacker authenticated entry toward control networks without touching a controller. Defenders should assume any internet-facing, unpatched NetScaler had its memory read after 1 July.

items.title: New “Armored Likho” APT hits electric-power and government targets with AI-generated malware
items.content_text: Kaspersky disclosed a previously undocumented espionage cluster it calls Armored Likho, targeting government agencies and electric-power sector organizations across Russia, Kazakhstan and Brazil. The intrusions start with spear-phishing that drops a RAR archive and staged loaders pulling a new Python infostealer, BusySnake, from GitHub — it harvests browser credentials, cookies, OTP codes and Telegram sessions and opens reverse-SSH tunnels for remote access. Notably, researchers assess the first-stage loaders were likely AI-generated, citing redundant comments and boilerplate code blocks inconsistent with hand-written malware — an early real-world signal of LLM-accelerated tooling reaching critical-infrastructure targeting. This is IT-side credential and espionage activity, not confirmed OT manipulation, but power-sector engineering staff are squarely in scope, and stolen credentials are the usual bridge from business networks into control systems.

items.title: First-ever in-the-wild exploitation of PTC Windchill hits a PLM platform embedded across manufacturing supply chains
items.content_text: Threat actors are exploiting CVE-2026-12569 in PTC Windchill and FlexPLM in the wild — the first confirmed real-world abuse of the product lifecycle management platform, and the first PTC flaw added to CISA's KEV catalog (added 25 June, federal deadline 28 June). The improper-input-validation bug lets a remote, unauthenticated attacker run arbitrary code; PTC says attackers are dropping persistent JSP webshells for command execution and data theft, and on 26 June warned of “heightened threat activity.” Windchill is embedded across automotive, aerospace, defense and heavy-machinery engineering, holding the design data, BOMs and process definitions that feed production — so a compromise threatens intellectual property and the integrity of what reaches the plant floor. PTC began shipping patches and IoCs on 17–18 June.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: KEV-added 25 Jun 2026 — exploitation confirmed 26 Jun; ongoing

items.title: Actively-exploited Lantronix EDS5000 flaw hands attackers root on a serial-to-IP bridge between PLCs and the enterprise network
items.content_text: CISA says CVE-2025-67038, a command-injection flaw in Lantronix EDS5000 serial-to-IP device servers, is being exploited in the wild and added it to the KEV catalog on 23 June (federal deadline 26 June, advisory ICSA-26-069-02). An unauthenticated attacker can inject OS commands via the username parameter and execute them as root, fully controlling a device whose entire job is bridging legacy serial gear — PLCs, RTUs, meters and sensors — onto IP networks. The flaw is one of 20 “BRIDGE:BREAK” issues Forescout disclosed in April; researchers showed such bugs can manipulate sensor readings to hide dangerous conditions. Forescout observed exploitation in an EDS5000 honeypot on 5 April — targeted fingerprinting, not commodity botnet traffic — suggesting attackers reverse-engineered the patch.

items.title: Cal Water finds no OT activity after Iran-linked Handala claimed it could disrupt the water supply
items.content_text: California Water Service (Cal Water) says its Mandiant-assisted investigation into a breach claimed by Iran-linked group Handala found no evidence of activity in its operational-technology environment, even though the actor asserted it could have disrupted water service but chose not to. Handala — widely believed to be a front for Iranian government operations — leaked roughly 5 GB of data; analysts found personal information and signs that a customer billing system and an internal application may have been touched. The pattern is now familiar: IT-side theft plus a loud OT-disruption claim used for psychological effect. The prudent response is to confirm OT integrity independently, assume claimed capabilities until disproven, and treat the incident as reconnaissance signaling against the water sector.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Reported 25 Jun 2026 — OT impact not found

items.title: “Blackfield” ransomware hits motor-maker Nidec’s Taiwan subsidiary, claiming 2 TB of manufacturing data and a $2M demand
items.content_text: Japanese industrial motor manufacturer Nidec confirmed ransomware-related damage on servers at its Taiwan-based subsidiary Nidec Chaun-Choung Technology, first detected 22 June. The Blackfield extortion group claims it stole more than two terabytes of corporate data — employee, financial, procurement, manufacturing, legal and IT records — and is demanding a $2 million ransom, having listed the victim with a leak-or-sell deadline. Nidec says the subsidiary runs on an independent network, which it believes contained the incident and kept it away from the broader corporation; there is no indication of OT or production-line disruption. For OT defenders the relevance is the pattern, not physical impact: manufacturers remain the top ransomware target, stolen engineering/procurement data aids follow-on targeting, and “independent network” assurances only hold if IT/OT segmentation is real and verified.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Detected 22 Jun 2026 — ransom demand late Jun / early Jul; ongoing

items.title: Dragos launches “EmberAI,” an OT-security AI assistant built on its industrial threat dataset
items.content_text: Dragos unveiled EmberAI, an AI capability built on its large operational-technology cybersecurity dataset and aimed at helping analysts triage and investigate industrial threats faster. The launch lands amid a broader shift: defenders are adopting AI assistants at roughly the same moment attackers are using LLMs to accelerate their own tooling — including, in Dragos's own casework this spring, an intrusion where an LLM guided reconnaissance toward OT assets at a water utility. The practical takeaway for asset owners is to evaluate OT-focused AI tools for genuine analyst leverage while keeping humans firmly in the loop for control-system decisions, and to scrutinize how any such tool handles sensitive OT telemetry before feeding it production data.

items.title: Accenture to take majority stake in Dragos in a $4.1B OT-security push, absorbing runZero and NetRise
items.content_text: Accenture agreed to acquire a majority stake in Dragos — valuing the industrial-cybersecurity firm at $3.25 billion — plus all of asset-discovery vendor runZero and firmware-security firm NetRise, roughly $4.1 billion combined, with runZero and NetRise to operate under Dragos. The deal is the latest in a consolidation wave that also includes ServiceNow's move for Armis and Dragos's own acquisition of xIoT firm Phosphorus. For asset owners the signal is strategic, not tactical: watch what services-giant ownership means for the independence of OT threat intelligence and product roadmaps, and factor vendor stability and integration risk into procurement and renewal decisions over the coming year.

items.title: Siemens urges immediate patching of RUGGEDCOM switches as a critical SINEC OS flaw (CVSS 9.8) draws a public exploit
items.content_text: CISA advisory ICSA-26-188-05 (published 7 July) flags a critical CVSS 9.8 vulnerability in Siemens SINEC OS versions before V4.0, the operating system running on RUGGEDCOM industrial networking gear. The root issue is a memory-corruption bug in a bundled third-party component (GNU elfutils) that a remote attacker can trigger to compromise unpatched devices; a public exploit has already been disclosed. RUGGEDCOM switches and routers are hardened OT networking backbone deployed in substations, rail, energy and heavy-industry environments, so a compromise sits directly in the path that segments and moves control-system traffic. Siemens has released a fixed version for the RUGGEDCOM RST2428P and urges customers to update immediately. No confirmed in-the-wild ICS attacks are reported yet, but with a public exploit and a 9.8 score on widely deployed OT infrastructure this warrants emergency patch prioritization.

items.title: CISA flags authentication and DoS flaws in Hydro-Québec’s Le Circuit Électrique EV-charging backend
items.content_text: CISA advisory ICSA-26-188-01 (published 7 July) covers vulnerabilities in the cloud backend of Hydro-Québec’s Le Circuit Électrique network, the platform managing more than 3,500 public EV charging stations across Quebec. The charging-station websocket endpoint accepts connections without proper authentication, which could allow privilege escalation, and there is no throttling on repeated authentication attempts to the backend, enabling a denial-of-service condition. No public exploitation is reported. EV-charging management platforms are an expanding slice of energy-sector OT that ties directly to grid load and public services, so weak backend authentication is a real exposure. Hydro-Québec has updated most stations to disable the OCPP protocol and added authentication for the remaining OCPP-reliant units to mitigate the risk.

items.title: Weak session tokens let attackers hijack management sessions across Schneider power relays, meters and RTUs
items.content_text: CISA advisory ICSA-26-169-07 covers CVE-2026-4827 (CVSS v4 8.2), an insufficient-entropy flaw that makes web-management session tokens predictable across a wide swath of Schneider Electric power gear — Easergy P3/P5 and T300 protection relays, EcoStruxure Power Monitoring Expert and Building Operation, PowerLogic ION and PM5000 meters, and Saitel DP/DR RTUs. A remote, unauthenticated attacker who observes a few tokens can predict valid ones and take over an authenticated console session, effectively bypassing login on devices that sit at the heart of substation protection, power monitoring and remote telemetry. No in-wild exploitation is reported yet, but the affected list spans equipment deployed across electric utilities and large facilities. Schneider has published firmware updates and mitigations; CISA urges prompt patching and tighter management-network isolation.

items.title: Hard-coded SNMP credentials in Schneider Easergy MiCOM Px40 protection relays expose substation device data
items.content_text: CISA advisory ICSA-26-190-03 (published 9 July) covers CVE-2026-4832, a use-of-hard-coded-credentials flaw in Schneider Electric's Easergy MiCOM Px40 protection-relay family used for medium-, high- and extra-high-voltage protection. An unauthenticated attacker who can reach the device's SNMP port can use the built-in credentials to read sensitive device information, aiding reconnaissance of substation protection schemes. Affected models include Easergy MiCOM P14x (before B4A), P24x (before D3A) and P341 (before E3F). No in-the-wild exploitation is reported, and impact is information disclosure rather than direct relay manipulation, but these relays sit at the heart of power protection, so exposed SNMP is a real recon foothold. Schneider has published fixed firmware; CISA urges minimizing network exposure and isolating relays behind firewalls off business networks.

items.title: Rockwell Automation patches vulnerabilities across Logix controllers, RSLinx and FactoryTalk
items.content_text: Rockwell Automation fixed security holes in its Logix, CompactLogix and Flex controllers and in RSLinx and FactoryTalk software — the controllers and connectivity/HMI tooling at the core of many discrete- and process-manufacturing lines. Specifics vary by advisory and none are reported exploited in the wild, but this is broadly deployed gear that sits deep inside production networks, where an unpatched flaw becomes an attractive foothold or a path to disrupt a process. Because controller and engineering-software updates often require planned downtime, the practical move is to map affected firmware and software versions to your asset inventory now and schedule patching against your maintenance windows, using network segmentation and tightened access to controllers and engineering workstations as interim controls.

items.title: Default admin credentials and path-traversal flaws let attackers hijack Daktronics highway-sign and billboard controllers
items.content_text: CISA advisory ICSA-26-176-04 covers three vulnerabilities a Princeton researcher found in Daktronics VFC-DMP-5000, DMP-5000 and DMP-8000 controllers — the units that drive the company’s large-scale LED displays on highways, airports, stadiums and metropolitan billboards. The chain combines an unauthenticated path-traversal bug for file enumeration and credential discovery, an authenticated arbitrary-file-upload flaw, and default administrator credentials that were never required to be changed; CISA warns successful exploitation can give an unauthenticated user complete root-level control. The researcher found many controllers exposed directly to the internet, most still running default creds, letting a remote attacker push false or malicious roadway/billboard messages or fully compromise the device. This is public-facing transportation and commercial-facility OT rather than core process control, but it is a real safety-messaging and public-trust risk. Daktronics has shipped patched firmware and urges customers to change default passwords and remove internet exposure.

items.title: NERC CIP 2026 milestones hit: control-center data protection now effective, internal network monitoring phasing in
items.content_text: A cluster of NERC CIP milestones is now live for electric utilities. CIP-012-2 became effective on 1 July 2026, strengthening protection of real-time operational data exchanged between control centers. Earlier in 2026, CIP-003-9 expanded governance for low-impact BES Cyber Systems around vendor electronic remote access and supply-chain risk, and CIP-015-1 introduced mandatory Internal Network Security Monitoring (INSM) with phased compliance running through 2030. Looking ahead, NERC must file a CIP-015-2 modification by 2 September 2026 to extend INSM scope to Electronic Access Control or Monitoring Systems (EACMS) and Physical Access Control Systems (PACS) outside the electronic security perimeter. Registered entities should confirm CIP-012-2 control-center protections are operating, stand up an INSM roadmap, and begin scoping EACMS/PACS assets ahead of the expansion.

items.title: EU NIS2: first compliance-audit deadline lands (30 June 2026) as incident reporting is standardized
items.content_text: The deadline for organizations to complete their first NIS2 compliance audit moved to 30 June 2026, and the EU has continued to standardize implementation — the NIS2 Cooperation Group adopted common incident-reporting templates and the Commission proposed simplifications to ease administrative burden while keeping notification timelines strict. For energy, water, manufacturing and other essential/important entities operating in or supplying the EU, the direction of travel is clear: broader scope, board-level accountability and faster, more standardized incident notification. Multinationals with OT footprints should confirm which entities and suppliers fall in scope, complete or validate their first audit, reconcile NIS2 obligations with NERC CIP and sector rules to avoid duplicative reporting, and pre-stage incident-reporting workflows against the common templates so regulatory clocks can be met under pressure.

items.title: CISA launches ANCHOR-CI, a new public-private framework for critical-infrastructure security collaboration
items.content_text: On 1 July, CISA announced ANCHOR-CI (Alliance of National Councils for Homeland Operational Resilience – Critical Infrastructure), an advisory-body framework meant to broaden information sharing and government-industry partnership on critical-infrastructure security. It absorbs lessons from the retired CIPAC model and can spin up four council types — sector, cross-sector, industry, and regional coordinating councils — drawing federal, state, local, tribal and territorial partners. It will run for an initial two years. For OT asset owners the practical value is a more structured channel for real-time threat sharing and for shaping sector guidance; the near-term move is to identify which council(s) map to your sector and region and to plug your ISAC/sector engagement into the new structure so you are inside the loop as it stands up.
